diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Expiratory airflow obstruction produces the sensation of not being able to push air out.
Widespread polyphonic expiratory wheeze is characteristic of diffuse bronchospasm.
Cardiac 'wheeze' can mimic asthma; requires cardiac features to distinguish.

symptoms.symptom_name: Reliever failure — diminishing response to repeated salbutamol

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Poor bronchodilator response marks severe, near-fatal asthma and impending fatigue.
Inability to complete sentences is a graded marker of severe asthma severity.
Recruitment of accessory muscles indicates high work of breathing from severe obstruction; visible to a carer on video.
Hypoxaemia from ventilation/perfusion mismatch; patient-obtainable so admissible in telehealth but unverified.
A primary panic attack does not cause true hypoxaemia — objective desaturation refutes the anxiety anchor.
Raised respiratory rate reflects the ventilatory response to airflow obstruction; a carer can count breaths, precise rate is clinician-measured.
Tachycardia reflects distress, hypoxia and beta-agonist effect; non-specific but consistent with severity.

symptoms.symptom_name: Polyphonic expiratory wheeze on auscultation with prolonged expiratory phase

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Diffuse polyphonic wheeze with prolonged expiration is characteristic; sealed clinician auscultation not obtainable via telehealth.
Exaggerated inspiratory fall in systolic pressure indicates severe obstruction; clinician-measured, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Reduced peak expiratory flow rate versus baseline

investigation_details.expected_result: Significantly reduced compared with the patient's personal best
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Loss of an objective severity/response measure; in telehealth the patient usually has no peak-flow meter, so severity is judged clinically.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Objective airflow limitation would confirm and grade severity; expected finding, not measurable via chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Respiratory alkalosis on arterial blood gas (early)

investigation_details.expected_result: Early respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation; a normalising or rising CO2 would signal fatigue and life-threatening deterioration
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: In hospital, a rising CO2 is a critical warning; unavailable in telehealth.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Gas exchange derangement supports severity and tracks fatigue; hospital investigation only.
No central cyanosis yet means not (currently) at the life-threatening/peri-arrest extreme — but this can change quickly and must be safety-netted.

symptoms.symptom_name: No basal crepitations, no raised JVP, no peripheral oedema

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of congestion signs makes cardiac wheeze/failure unlikely; clinician exam finding, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: No allergen exposure, urticaria or angioedema

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No trigger, hives or facial/airway swelling and gradual onset exclude anaphylaxis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Poor preventer (inhaled corticosteroid) adherence over the last month

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Under-treated airway inflammation predisposes to severe exacerbation on a viral trigger; a modifiable driver.
Viral infection is the most common trigger of acute asthma exacerbations.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Reliever failure + speech limited to short phrases + accessory-muscle use + hypoxia together define acute severe (near-life-threatening) asthma and mandate emergency escalation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Track oxygenation; a fall below 92% indicates life-threatening asthma needing controlled oxygen.
Objective severity and treatment-response measure where a meter is available; usually unavailable to a telehealth patient.
In the emergency setting, a rising CO2 signals fatigue and impending respiratory failure.
Consider if not improving or to exclude pneumothorax/pneumonia; not routine for a typical exacerbation.
Not first-line for acute asthma; ordering it would be low-yield over-investigation that delays treatment.
PE is not clinically suspected here; reflex D-dimer/CTPA would be an over-workup error unless PE features emerge.
